cystine transporter solute carrier family 3 member 2

Ferroptosis—The "Double-Edged Sword" in Cancer: Mechanisms of Tumor Suppression/Resistance and Therapeutic Manipulation

evidence_entities.text: Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of programmed cell death that is driven by the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to the formation of membrane-damaging lipid peroxides
Morphologically, cells undergoing ferroptosis exhibit several changes in their mitochondria, including smaller size, decreased cristae, and much more condensed membranes
Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent process, as iron is obligatory for this form of cell death to occur. Iron in its ferrous state (Fe2+) is required for the Fenton reaction

TP53 is the most frequently mutated tumor-suppressor gene and can play an important role in the promotion of ferroptosis in cancer. It downregulates SLC7A11 expression, a key component of the xCT system

Erastin acts through the inhibition of the xCT antiporter composed of both SLC7A11 and SLC3A2. Inhibition of this antiporter leads to decreased intracellular cystine uptake and subsequent depletion of GSH
RSL3 is another induction molecule that directly inhibits GPX4 by bypassing the need for GSH depletion
Dendritic cells failed to effectively present tumor antigens as they undergo ferroptosis, resulting in weakened CD8+ T cell responses

notes: This paper is a narrative review focusing on ferroptosis mechanisms in cancer
The paper discusses both tumor-suppressive and tumor-promoting roles of ferroptosis
Multiple therapeutic strategies targeting ferroptosis are described
The immune system interactions with ferroptosis are extensively covered
